metadata.paper_title: A Case Study in Performance, Modifiability and Extensibility Analysis of a Telecommunication System Software Architecture
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the 10th IEEE Int'l Symp. on Modeling, Analysis, & Simulation of Computer & Telecommunications Systems (MASCOTS'02)

system_context.system_description: A system offering voice, data and multimedia services over the company's Intranet, providing a global solution for communication in the enterprise environment.
system_context.business_goals: To provide a global solution for the communication in the enterprise environment; increase the offering of services for enterprise communications.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To evaluate two alternative software architectures with respect to extensibility, performance, and modifiability, addressing problems of the existing system.

architectures.name: H.323 Architecture
architectures.description: The existing architecture based on ITU-T H.323 protocol. It uses Terminal Agents to allow H.323 terminals to access the system and a SiteKeeper for routing and resource management.

key_components.responsibilities: Allows H.323 terminals to access the system; multiplexes message requests and responses.
Implements user system functionalities (Authentication, Registration, Call handling, Service Triggering).
Interface between SN and access provider; performs routing for calls and resources management.
Performs call control functions (Address Translation, Admission Control, Bandwidth Control).

architectures.name: SIP Architecture
architectures.description: A new architecture based on IETF SIP protocol. It introduces a Multiprotocol Access Controller (MAC) for protocol translation and uses SIP Proxies and Registrars.

key_components.responsibilities: Access point for terminals; provides protocol translation to HLTP and then to SIP (UAC/UAS level).
Performs terminal location, message sorting; Call Manager component manages SIP calls and user administration.
Manages the registration of SIP terminals.

scenarios.scenario_text: Basic call (comprising Discovery, Registration, Call Setup, Capabilities Exchange, Call Release, Unregistration)
Virtual Private Network / Private Numbering Plan (VPN/PNP) insertion

scenario_classification.rationale: Represents the core functionality of the telecommunication system (making calls).
Categorized as 'Extension of capabilities' requiring architectural modification.

issues_identified: Capabilities Exchange requires 8 components and 4 messages.
High number of exchanged messages (performance problem).
Discovery requires 0 components and 0 messages (phase not specified in SIP).
Capabilities Exchange requires 0 messages (integrated in Call Setup).
Impossible to understand which components have to be changed.
Impossible to understand which components have to be changed.

scenario_interactions.modularity_implication: These services all require modification of the same specific components (UAC/UAS in MAC and CM in Proxy), indicating a clear localization of changes but potential coupling for these specific features.

architectural_strengths: SIP architecture performs better (fewer components and messages involved in basic call phases).
SIP architecture allows for easier extension of supplementary services (clear specification of affected components).
Centralization of Access Agent in the MAC makes the system more open to different terminals.
HLTP level divides external protocols from internal protocol, increasing modifiability.
architectural_weaknesses: H.323 architecture has expensive call setup and strict use of transport layer (TCP).
H.323 architecture shows 'High' difficulty for inserting UCT, CH, and ACT services.
H.323 architecture binary codification reduces error detection.

architecture_comparison.rationale: Demonstrated to be more modifiable and extensible; guarantees better performances (fewer messages/components); clear identification of components for new services.
Difficult to add new supplementary services (violations of time-to-market); performance problems due to high number of exchanged messages.

recommendations: Adoption of the SIP protocol architecture.
Use the proposed architecture as a pattern for systems with similar performance and extensibility requirements.

missing_information: Full list of scenarios (only examples and basic call phases provided)
inference_notes: Inferred 'Indirect' classification for supplementary services based on the analysis of 'Insertion Difficulty'.
Inferred 'Direct' classification for Basic Call as it is the primary function.
